briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon. I will now begin the climate briefing for April 17, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Deepening Signs of Climate Crisis and Widespread Impacts
sections.content: Climate-related news released today warns that the global climate crisis is intensifying. Firstly, concerns have been raised about the possibility of a 'Super El Niño' [2, 72], and domestically, a phenomenon of 'dangerous diurnal temperature range' where temperatures fluctuate like stock prices is being observed [3]. Such climate changes are altering the 'spring flower map' by accelerating the blooming period of spring flowers, including not only cherry blossoms but also acacia and rowan trees [4]. Research has also revealed that wildfires are exhibiting different patterns than in the past, such as 'burning overnight' even at night, and their activity is becoming more intense [47, 52, 54, 58, 59, 60, 61, 62, 63, 67]. In particular, the warning that up to 90% of the world's coral reefs are endangered suggests a severe threat to ecosystems [70], and the impacts on various species and industries, such as adaptation issues for endangered mice and worsening diseases in Norwegian salmon farms, are also noteworthy [43, 49, 55].

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Realities and Challenges of Domestic Carbon Neutrality and Renewable Energy Transition
sections.content: Domestically, various practical efforts to respond to the climate crisis are ongoing, but at the same time, several challenges are being faced. The Korea Climate Change Institute has declared its leap towards becoming an action-oriented organization [5], and various local governments, including Mokpo City, Incheon Jung-gu, Yangju City, and Pocheon City, encouraged carbon neutrality practices by operating Climate Change Week and a '10-minute lights-out campaign' in commemoration of 'Earth Day' [6, 7, 13, 14, 16, 17, 18]. At the corporate level, Hyundai Rotem is accelerating its response to carbon neutrality by developing energy-saving technologies for railway vehicles [19], and Siheung City is exploring a transition to a renewable energy city, including commencing analysis of urban wind power generation [25, 28]. However, criticism has emerged that Busan is taking a 'turtle's pace' in the speed of its renewable energy transition [21], and despite the proposal of a bill to expand public renewable energy through the establishment of 'Korea Power Generation Corporation,' opposition from labor unions has been raised, arguing that energy transition does not guarantee job transition, making it urgent to find solutions [22, 23].

sections.title: 🌍 International Climate Response Efforts and Political Turbulence
sections.content: The international community is responding to climate change in various ways, but political conflicts and policy turbulence also persist. Vietnam's Nghe An Province is discussing climate change adaptation urban development projects with the World Bank [8], and international cooperation is active, with KOICA and GGGI attracting investments to strengthen Vietnam's carbon neutrality [10]. A community in Colombia has assisted the entire city's climate change plan through rainwater harvesting and eco-gardens [33, 71], and an international governmental organization has assessed China as leading the world in climate change response [51]. On the other hand, in the United States, despite concerns about the climate crisis being at an all-time high, society appears divided [9, 36], and policy discord is evident, with the military continuing its preparations despite the US Secretary of Defense's climate change skepticism [34, 45]. Furthermore, the US Supreme Court's ruling in favor of oil companies in climate change lawsuits could serve as a significant obstacle to achieving climate justice [48, 69].

sections.title: 📰 Economic and Industrial Changes Triggered by Climate Change
sections.content: Climate change is demanding new analyses and responses across the economy and industry. On Wall Street, financial markets have begun to recognize climate factors as a key variable, even analyzing climate change during planting seasons [1]. South Korea views achieving carbon neutrality as a national 'challenge and opportunity' and is seeking changes in its industrial structure [15], and in the EU, new funding mechanisms are being explored, including discussions on whether the introduction of a 'windfall tax' could be a breakthrough for renewable energy transition [32]. These movements demonstrate that climate change is no longer limited to environmental issues but is becoming a core driver demanding a fundamental restructuring of economic systems.

briefing.closing: Today's briefing has once again highlighted the severity of the climate crisis, while also demonstrating the numerous political and economic challenges that must be addressed alongside various domestic and international response efforts. Continued attention and effort are urgently needed. This concludes today's climate briefing.

articles.title: Supreme court sides with oil and gas firms in Louisiana coastal damage fight
articles.description: 8-0 ruling gives companies new day in federal court after firms including Chevron ordered to pay millions for cleanupThe supreme court handed a win on Friday to oil and gas companies fighting lawsuits over coastal land loss and environmental degradation in Louisiana.The 8-0 procedural decision gives the companies a new day in federal court after a state jury ordered Chevron to pay upward of $740m to clean up damage to the state’s coastline, one of multiple similar lawsuits. Continue reading...

summary.phenomenon: The U.S. Supreme Court issued an 8-0 procedural ruling in favor of oil and gas companies in lawsuits concerning coastal land loss and environmental degradation in Louisiana. This decision grants companies, including Chevron, a new opportunity in federal court after a state jury had ordered them to pay millions of dollars for cleanup.
summary.outlook: Oil and gas companies will be granted a new opportunity in federal court.

articles.title: Coral reefs are nearing extinction. 2026 must mark a turning point | Jason Momoa
articles.description: At 1.5C of global warming, up to 90% of coral reefs could be lost. The next few months could be a defining momentWhere I come from – Hawai’i – the reef isn’t just something you look at. It’s part of us. It feeds our families, protects our shores, and lives at the center of our culture. In our stories, coral is one of our oldest ancestors. It’s a reminder that everything in the ocean, and all of us, are connected.Right now, that integral connection is under threat.Jason Momoa is an actor, film-ma

summary.phenomenon: Up to 90% of global coral reefs could be lost at 1.5°C of global warming, and coral reefs are currently endangered. (Hawaiian coral reefs serve as a cultural center, food source, and coastal protection.)
summary.cause: 1.5°C of global warming is identified as the cause of the coral reef endangerment. (Specific scientific causes/background are not mentioned in the article.)
summary.outlook: The next few months could be a defining moment for the fate of coral reefs, and 2026 should be a turning point.

articles.original_title: Rainwater harvesting and eco-gardens: how one Colombian neighbourhood helped a whole city plan for climate change
articles.description: From rainwater harvesting to tree nurseries, communities in Medellín are taking steps to increase their landslide and flooding resilienceIn his home on a steep hillside in the neighbourhood of Golondrinas in Medellín, Róbinson Velásquez Cartagena stands proudly next to two large tanks of water – a rainwater harvesting system he designed and built to help reduce the risk of flooding and landslides.It is one of the nature-based solutions that Velásquez and others in the community have proposed as

summary.phenomenon: Residents of Golondrinas, Medellín, Colombia, are implementing nature-based solutions such as rainwater harvesting systems and tree nurseries. These measures aim to reduce the risk of flooding and landslides.
summary.outlook: These solutions are expected to contribute to increasing the community's resilience to landslides and floods.
